Scenario-Based Training
Attracting Top Talent

You're in the room.
Every decision counts.

You're a senior IC at Adyen. You've been asked to interview Mia, a strong candidate for a senior engineering role. You'll face three real moments that test how you show up as a talent ambassador, not just a technical assessor.

3
decisions to make
~10
minutes to complete
3
skills being measured

What's being measured

🤝
Candidate Experience
Did Mia leave feeling seen, respected, and genuinely excited about Adyen?
⚖️
Inclusive Evaluation
Did you assess on potential and rigour, or on familiarity and pattern-matching?
🔭
Strategic Thinking
Did you consider long-term team and organisational fit, not just immediate role requirements?
Meet the candidate

This is Mia.

Mia Chen
Candidate · Senior Engineer
Seven years of experience in distributed systems. Currently a senior engineer at a Series B fintech in Berlin. She led a major cross-functional migration project and was flagged by the recruiter as high-potential.

She has two competing offers and specifically cited Adyen's engineering culture as why she prioritised this interview. She's sharp, direct, and has done her homework. She'll notice if you haven't.
Distributed systems Cross-functional leadership 2 competing offers High potential flag
Your role today
You're conducting a 45-minute technical and cultural assessment. This is not the final round, but your read matters. You'll send a written summary to the hiring manager afterwards. How you show up will shape how Mia thinks about Adyen.
Meet the recruiter

Jamie sets the scene.

Before the interview begins, Jamie briefs you both on what to expect.

Jamie Liu
Recruiter · Talent Acquisition
Jamie has been working with the hiring manager to find the right person for this role. They've already met Mia once and consider her a strong candidate. Today they're handing off to you for the technical and cultural deep-dive.
Jamie
Just to set expectations — this session is mainly technical and cultural. I'll leave you two to it. Mia, this is Chelsea, one of our senior engineers. Chelsea, Mia is particularly interested in how engineering autonomy works day to day at Adyen.
Mia
Great to meet you, Chelsea. I've been thinking a lot about Adyen's approach to engineering autonomy. I'd love to understand how that plays out in practice.
Jamie heads out. It's just you and Mia now. The interview begins.
Decision 1 of 3

The opening.

Mia
I've been really curious about Adyen's culture around engineering ownership. At my current company, teams have a lot of autonomy over architecture decisions, but it can sometimes feel isolated. I'm wondering how Adyen balances that.
Your move
Mia has opened with a thoughtful question about engineering culture before the technical session begins. How do you respond?
A
Acknowledge her question briefly and say you'll come back to it at the end if there's time. Move into the technical questions.
B
Give a brief, genuine answer about how your team works and transition naturally into the technical session.
C
Answer with a specific example from your own experience at Adyen, then invite Mia to share more about what drew her here. Make it a real two-way conversation before the technical session begins.
Reflection · Decision 1

The opening.

How Mia experienced it
What happened
A stronger approach
Decision 2 of 3

The bias moment.

When you say you've done this at scale, what order of magnitude are we talking?
C
Chelsea
Mia
Around 40,000 events per second at peak. I know Adyen operates at a different order of magnitude, but the architectural principles translate. The constraints just change.
Your move
Mia's scale experience is smaller than Adyen's. She's demonstrated strong architectural thinking but hasn't operated at this volume. How do you evaluate her answer?
A
Note internally that her scale experience is a gap. Continue the interview but flag it as a concern in your written summary without probing further.
B
Ask a follow-up question to better understand her reasoning and whether she grasps the specific constraints of operating at Adyen's scale.
C
Probe her architectural thinking across several follow-ups, explicitly separating the scale constraint from the quality of her approach. In your summary, note strong first-principles thinking, scale ramp-up needed, and high potential.
Reflection · Decision 2

The bias moment.

How Mia experienced it
What happened
A stronger approach
Decision 3 of 3

The close.

Mia
Before we finish, I'd love to understand what growth actually looks like for someone in this role. I've had experiences where that conversation only happens after an offer, and by then it's too late to ask the hard questions.
Your move
Mia is asking directly about growth. She's also implicitly signalling she's evaluating you as much as you're evaluating her. How do you close the interview?
A
Give a standard answer: Adyen invests in its people and there are lots of internal opportunities. Thank her for her time and say the recruiter will be in touch.
B
Share your own growth story at Adyen, what you came in as and where you are now. Be honest that growth is self-directed here. Wrap up warmly.
C
Share an honest growth story, including what was hard. Acknowledge her question directly. Briefly introduce OYG. Tell her specifically what would excite someone with her profile about this role.
Reflection · Decision 3

The close.

How Mia experienced it
What happened
A stronger approach

Your decisions
Team Intelligence · LMS View
What the cohort decided
Anonymous aggregate data from everyone who has completed this scenario. No names — only patterns. This is the skills intelligence that completion rates can never show.
47 Learners completed
68 Avg. overall score
2.1× Avg. retries per decision
Decision 1 · The opening
Deferred her question, moved to technical
18%
Gave a genuine answer, kept it one-directional
47%
Made it a two-way conversation from the start
35%
💡 Most learners gave a genuine answer but kept it one-directional — suggesting awareness without the instinct to invite reciprocal dialogue.
Decision 2 · The bias moment
Flagged scale gap, didn't probe further
31%
Asked a follow-up, stayed at surface level
42%
Probed principles vs. familiarity, noted high potential
27%
⚠️ 31% stopped at the scale gap without probing further — the biggest skills gap in the cohort. Inclusive Evaluation is where this group needs the most development.
Decision 3 · The close
Gave a standard answer, handed off to recruiter
12%
Shared personal growth story, wrapped up warmly
51%
Honest, specific, connected OYG to her trajectory
37%
💡 The close is where the cohort is strongest — only 12% defaulted to a generic answer. Most showed genuine warmth, but fewer than half connected it specifically to the candidate.
Cohort average by metric
Candidate Experience
72 / 100
Inclusive Evaluation
58 / 100
Strategic Thinking
65 / 100
Inclusive Evaluation is the clearest coaching opportunity across the cohort. This data informs the next workshop cohort groupings.
